class-files-and-instances.label: Class Files and Instances
class-files-and-instances.summary: Create `models/account.py`, define a class shell with `pass`, import it into `main.py`, and create one object cleanly.

sketch1.title: Import the class and create one object

sketch2.title: Keep the model file separate from the main script

try_class_files_and_instances_sketch0.title: Define the Account class shell
try_class_files_and_instances_sketch0.prompt: In `models/account.py`, define `class Account:` with `pass` inside it. Do not add a constructor yet. Do not add attributes or methods yet. `main.py` can stay unchanged in this first step.
try_class_files_and_instances_sketch0.hint: This lesson is only about making the class exist in its own file.

help.concept: `pass` means the class exists, but it does not do any work yet. That is enough for the first clean class shell.

# The class definition belongs in models/account.py.
# You do not need to add code in main.py yet.

main_py.content:
# The class definition belongs in models/account.py.
# You do not need to add code in main.py yet.

# The class definition belongs in models/account.py.

0.message: Define an Account class in models/account.py.

1.message: The class shell should be constructible with no arguments yet.

try_class_files_and_instances_sketch1.title: Import Account and create one object
try_class_files_and_instances_sketch1.prompt: Keep `models/account.py` as a class shell with `pass`. In `main.py`, import `Account` from `models.account` and create one object with `account = Account()`.
try_class_files_and_instances_sketch1.hint: The model file defines the class. The main file uses it.

help.concept: A common layout is: model file defines the class, main file imports it, then main creates objects.

try_class_files_and_instances_sketch1.starterCode:
# Import Account from models.account.
# Then create one object with account = Account().

main_py.content:
# Import Account from models.account.
# Then create one object with account = Account().

from models.account import Account

account = Account()

try_class_files_and_instances_sketch1.solutionCode:
from models.account import Account

account = Account()

0.message: Keep the Account class defined in the model file.

2.message: Create one Account object in main.py.

try_class_files_and_instances_sketch2.title: Keep the model file separate and main.py thin
try_class_files_and_instances_sketch2.prompt: Keep the `Account` class in `models/account.py`. In `main.py`, import it, create `checking_account = Account()`, and print `type(checking_account).__name__` so the main script shows that it is using the model cleanly.
try_class_files_and_instances_sketch2.hint: Do not move the class into main.py.

help.concept: Keeping the class in its own file helps the main script stay focused on using objects instead of defining them.
help.hint_1: Import the class instead of rewriting it in main.py.
help.hint_2: Printing `type(checking_account).__name__` gives you one visible result without adding new attributes or methods yet.

try_class_files_and_instances_sketch2.starterCode:
# Keep the class in models/account.py.
# Import it here, create one object, and print the class name.

main_py.content:
# Keep the class in models/account.py.
# Import it here, create one object, and print the class name.

from models.account import Account

checking_account = Account()
print(type(checking_account).__name__)

try_class_files_and_instances_sketch2.solutionCode:
from models.account import Account

checking_account = Account()
print(type(checking_account).__name__)

0.message: Keep the Account class defined in models/account.py.

2.message: Create one Account object in the main script.

mc-class-files-and-instances-class-role.title: What is the class doing here?
mc-class-files-and-instances-class-role.prompt: Which statements are correct in this topic? Choose all that apply.
mc-class-files-and-instances-class-role.hint: One answer is about the class definition, and another is about the main script using that class.

help.concept: In this topic, the class lives in the model file and the main script imports it to create objects.
help.hint_1: Pick the answers that match that file split.
help.hint_2: Reject answers that say the object is created automatically by the import.

options.b: `main.py` should usually define the full class in this topic.
options.c: `main.py` can import the class and create an object from it.
options.d: Using `pass` means the class can never be expanded later.

sc-class-files-and-instances-pass-meanin.title: What does `pass` mean here?
sc-class-files-and-instances-pass-meanin.prompt: Why is `pass` useful inside the first `Account` class shell?
sc-class-files-and-instances-pass-meanin.hint: It lets the class exist before it does real work.

help.concept: `pass` is a placeholder that tells Python the class exists even though you have not added behavior yet.
help.hint_1: Look for the answer about an empty but valid class body.
help.hint_2: Reject answers about printing or importing.

options.a: It automatically creates the object's attributes
options.b: It keeps the empty class valid until you add more later
options.d: It turns the class into a function

fb-class-files-and-instances-import-name.title: Complete the import
fb-class-files-and-instances-import-name.prompt: Fill in the missing class name.
fb-class-files-and-instances-import-name.hint: Use the class you defined in the model file.

help.concept: The import line names the class you want to use in the main script.
help.hint_1: The module path is already complete.
help.hint_2: The missing word is the class name itself.

dr-class-files-and-instances-order.title: Order the workflow
dr-class-files-and-instances-order.prompt: Put these steps in the best order for this topic.
dr-class-files-and-instances-order.hint: The class must exist before main.py can import it.

help.concept: The normal order is: define the class, import it, create an object, then use it.
help.hint_1: Importing comes after the class exists in its own file.
help.hint_2: Using the object comes after it has been created.

tokens.t4: Print or inspect the created object

sketch-0.title: Start with a class shell
sketch-0.bodyMarkdown:
Now that objects feel familiar, we can write the smallest possible class ourselves.

```python
class Account:
    pass
```

This class does not store anything yet. It only says, "an `Account` class exists."

`pass` is a placeholder. It keeps Python happy until we add real code later.

sketch-1.title: Import the class and create one object
sketch-1.bodyMarkdown:
A clean beginner layout keeps the class in one file and the program logic in another.

```python
# models/account.py
class Account:
    pass
```

```python
# main.py
from models.account import Account

account = Account()
```

Even without attributes yet, this proves the files are connected correctly.

sketch-2.title: Keep the model file separate from the main script
sketch-2.bodyMarkdown:
`models/account.py` should define the class.

`main.py` should stay thin and mostly do jobs like these:

- import the class
- create objects
- print results

That separation makes later topics easier when the class starts growing.
